column1: 7,IssuesEvent,2017-04-07 11:19:39+00:00,7470121,wuub/rxv,506181,tari,,,opened,Crash on detecting N560,[],[],0,"I have a CRX-N560D on my network that crashes rxv when trying to detect devices because it doesn't define a `controlURL` or `unitDescURL`. `ssdp.rxv_discover` fails when it tries to take the text content of these elements that don't exist.

A simple workaround is to check whether the calls to `xml.find` succeed and bail out without throwing an exception if they fail. That said, I'd prefer to add support for this device- it uses a similar but incompatible protocol that I expect can be fit into the existing API.
* An abstract class defining the public API (`Receiver` perhaps) that implements common parts of the protocol.
* A subclass with the current implementation for currently-supported devices (`RXV`).
* Another subclass that implements the same API for the N560 and related devices.
* Some kind of device detection in `find` to instantiate the correct subclass for a detected device.

The feature set for a public API differs mostly in that the N560 has no sense of zones- the code may act as though there is one with a fixed name at all times. Internally, there appears to be a fixed set of inputs and no way to query which are available so the supported operations for each input need to be known statically (relatively easy, but probably implies a model number check in initialization).
For reference, the desc.xml returned during discovery:

26,IssuesEvent,2016-06-18 11:15:37+00:00,2918192,epsy/clize,685865,epsy,,,opened,Ensure sphinx interoperability,"[{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/epsy/clize/labels/feature%20request', 'name': 'feature request', 'color': '009800'}]",[],0,"Clize is geared towards letting code still be used as code, but the legacy of clize's docstring format impairs this.
Most notably, they cannot be properly parsed by sphinx.ext.autodoc as it is. A sphinx extension could be devised for this, but it is more preferable for Clize to support sphinx/docutils fields.
Special care needs to be given towards the following:
- Features of Clize docstrings such as customizable ordering and naming sections for named options must be preserved.
It could be acceptable to diminish the flexibility of sections to just one per decorator.
Optionally, a directive could be added to format a function to be documented as a command-line interface.
Also, sphinx's contributed napoleon extension could be used to support Google- and Numpy-style docstrings.",{},{}

751,IssuesEvent,2015-03-04 17:06:59+00:00,4689577,cjdrake/pyeda,1093960,cjdrake,,,closed,Implement ANF and NNF,"[{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/cjdrake/pyeda/labels/Change', 'name': 'Change', 'color': '207de5'}]",[],1,"Currently, the `boolalg.expr` module implements conjunctive normal form (CNF), and disjunctive normal form (DNF). There are two other normal forms for sale on the interwebs.

The [Algebraic Normal Form](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebraic_normal_form) is an `XOR` of `AND` of variables. It relies heavily on properties of distribution over `XOR`, and the identity `~x <=> XOR(1, x)`. Given how simplification works, this normal form might be best implemented as a `set` of `frozenset` of `int`. Bonus that it's a canonical form!

Apparently, pyeda's ""factored"" form is also called [Negation Normal Form](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negation_normal_form), or NNF. I originally got the term ""factor"" from DeMicheli's [Synthesis and Optimization of Digital Circuits](http://www.amazon.com/Synthesis-Optimization-Digital-Circuits-Giovanni/dp/0070163332). However, the term ""factoring"" is also used to describe a mathematical technique used during multi-level logic minimization, so a good idea to disambiguate. Proposal would be to change the `factor` method to `to_nnf`.

878,IssuesEvent,2018-05-19 15:11:17+00:00,16964283,olofk/fusesoc,1467123,imphil,,,opened,"CAPI2: Rename ""toplevel"" to plural form",[],[],0,"CAPI2 files expect in the ""toplevel"" key an array of strings representing toplevel modules. An example can be found here: https://github.com/optimsoc/optimsoc/blob/spyglass/examples/fpga/nexys4ddr/compute_tile/compute_tile_nexys4ddr_capi2.core#L52
That's counterintuitive in a couple of ways:
- Passing a string makes the parser create a list of individual letters, certainly not what people expect.
- The key name ""toplevel"" indicates a single value, while a list is expected.
I'm therefore proposing to rename the key to ""toplevels"". This makes it clear that a list of elements is expected.
Another option would be to make toplevel expect really a string (actually the most common case), and have a separate option ""toplevels"" with the ability to specify multiple toplevel modules.",{},{}

First of all I want to say that you guys are working on a really great product which has much practical use for me!

I making a program which uses the keyboard library, but I stumbled upon a problem which I spent quite some time debugging. I hope this issue is not a duplicate, I didn't look very far in the list of reported issues.
Problem description: when I bind 'h' as a hotkey and then press the 'backspace' key, the 'h' hotkey gets handled.
How to reproduce: Run the code below (and don't touch your keyboard for 0.1sec), if the program prints something to console you reproduced the bug.
def h_handler():print(""This should not be handled"")
sleep(0.1) # give event manager time to handle keypress
The 'h' key get assigned correctly (35) but gets overwritten by a wrong value at _nixkeyboard.py:97
This is caused by the usage of synonyms from `dumpkeys --long-info` (_nixkeyboard.py:92).
This is what I see in my shell, these are some of the synonyms:
the `cleanup_key()` function yields 'h' when 'Control_h' is provided as input. Causing to map 'h' to backspace.

The output of `dumpkeys` is not strange, Control_h (= Ctrl+h) is a synonym for backspace as this article says: http://wiki.linuxquestions.org/wiki/List_of_keysyms. Control_l and Control_j also have synonyms. The other Control_a through Control_g and Control_k through_z do not have a synonym. I assume these synonyms are a linux thing as I do not recognize these key mappings in the Windows OSes.
On my machine the following synonyms get mapped in `from_name` at _nixkeyboard.py:97

(h,backspace) (i,tab) (j,enter) (home,find) (end,select) (page up,page up) (page down,page down) (multiplication,*) (ø,Ø) (altl,alt) (altr,alt gr) (alt l,alt) (alt r,alt gr) (altgr l,alt) (altgr r,alt gr) (spawn console,keyboardsignal) (~,~) (^,asciicircum) (ogonek,,) (caron,^) (breve,~) (doubleacute,~) (paragraph sign,§)
(There are more synonyms but they don't get inserted because the `original` key does not exist in `from_name`, causing an exception. A complete list: https://pastebin.com/EBpc1JYH).

Observing these synonyms makes me wonder what they are for. Why would we want to map these synonyms? And why do these synonyms have priority over older mappings? (It has priority because it overwrites previous data)
I hope somone could shed some light on it.

2404,IssuesEvent,2017-03-09 14:34:00+00:00,5934517,dpkp/kafka-python,4463898,declantraynor,,,opened,Suspected memory leak in consumer when using lz4 compression ,[],[],0,I've noticed a problem with unbounded memory usage in the consumer when handling lz4-compressed payloads. The symptoms point to a memory leak of some description. I've created a [test case](https://gist.github.com/declantraynor/88a1477c7430601f6e14af1c2998effe) which should allow easy reproduction of the issue.

I've tried experimenting with various consumer settings such as `fetch_max_bytes `, `max_partition_fetch_bytes` and `max_in_flight_requests_per_connection` in an attempt to lessen the buffering requirements of each individual request to Kafka. In all cases, memory usage of consumer processes has continued to rise until such a point that those processes are killed by the host system.

I can additionally confirm that this issue is present in the latest PyPI release (1.3.2) as well as `master` and that the issue only manifests when using `lz4` compression specifically. `gzip` appears to be working fine, for example.
Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks!",{},{}

3081,IssuesEvent,2016-08-30 21:30:33+00:00,33454782,tkem/mopidy-dleyna,43726,jodal,,,opened,Please check for XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/bus before running dbus-launch,[],[],0,Quoting https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=836117:
> I'm trying to reduce how much dbus-launch is used in Debian.
> One issue with this approach is that it second-guesses how the D-Bus
> client implementation itself will find the session bus. In recent libdbus
> and GDBus, the fallback behaviour if DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS is unset
> is to look for $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/bus: if the environment variable is
> set, that directory contains ./bus, and ./bus is a socket owned by
> the current uid, then libdbus and GDBus will automatically use it. In
> particular, the dbus-user-session Debian package sets up this situation.
> mopidy-dleyna should not run dbus-launch without first looking
> Another issue with the approach this package has taken is that it relies
> on dbus-launch, which is X11-specific legacy code that does several
> things, none of them particularly well.
> This Flatpak commit illustrates how eval `dbus-launch` can be replaced
> by invoking dbus-daemon directly, avoiding the X11-specific dbus-launch:
> It is possible to send both the address and the pid to stdout
> (""--print-address=1 --print-pid=1"") if that would be easier to deal
> with; their order is undocumented but predictable, and I don't intend
> to break it in future D-Bus releases.
> Alternatively, and perhaps most simply, this package could stop trying to
> compensate for a missing session bus address at all. On systems with
> $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/bus, it would ""just work"" anyway; or when run under X11
> (even with no dbus-daemon running), X11 autolaunching would create a
> dbus-daemon anyway; or if it is being run in a non-GUI environment like
> cron, its documentation could mention that this may require using
> which has been available since dbus 1.8, and automatically cleans up
> the dbus-daemon after COMMAND terminates (successfully or not).
> This is really an upstream bug. If you forward it upstream, please
> quote the full text of this bug report and let upstream make their

return f(self, *args, **kwargs)  # Call the callback for the event

4159,IssuesEvent,2016-10-05 15:24:12+00:00,8557809,FGRibreau/mailchecker,138050,FGRibreau,,,closed,list.json uses non-standard syntax - could be formatted also for easier parsing,[],[],1,"I tried to implement a Golang platform but got stuck at how the list.json file is formatted. It uses non-standard single-line comments. And it is formatted as an array of arrays with strings. To be more compliant I suggest it should be a array of strings, where every string is on a seperate line.

I have already drafted a golang library generator script but parsing this is currently a pain. Checking for duplicates is also much easier then (with unix tools) if every domain string is in on a separate file.

4408,IssuesEvent,2017-04-19 21:36:40+00:00,52575367,nficano/python-lambda,2015907,kzuberi,,,opened,including executable binaries,[],[],0,"Lambda supports execution of binary programs within a lambda function, and python-lambda is a convenient way to package and upload these wrapped in a python interface. This *almost* works perfectly as is, except that python zip doesn't preserve executable bits when creating the package, and AWS requires these to be set or else the binary will fail with a permissions error.

I worked around this for my own use case by hacking [helpers.py](https://github.com/nficano/python-lambda/blob/master/aws_lambda/helpers.py#L19) based on code in [this stackexchange question](https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/14705/the-zip-formats-external-file-attribute), hardcoding special handling for my executable file. A more complete solution might be to check the executable flags on the files when packaging and preserve when building the zip archive, or alternatively allow the config file to specify  a list of executables for special handling during packaging.

1) in your exception-raiser code you don't set the errno with the unqlite return code so an exception handler needs to examine the message - but this not easy to do because there is more than one message for a single unqlite error condition. You are forced to look through the .c file and extract strings, etc. Not much fun.

4988,IssuesEvent,2017-03-31 19:41:34+00:00,4523552,mattrobenolt/jinja2-cli,12202985,jefftp,,,opened,keep_trailing_newline not configurable?,[],[],0,"keep_trailing_newline is True by default with jinja2-cli, which is not the prescribed default configuration of Jinja2 (http://jinja.pocoo.org/docs/2.9/templates/#whitespace-control). There doesn't appear to be a way to alter this behavior short of modifying the code?
The issue at hand is that this makes one particular scenario a huge challenge: conditionals with whitespace sensitive output. For example,
Keep the blank below this line.
Keep the blank above this line.

Without any ""-"" for whitespace control, you get added newlines. If you add a trailing ""-"" to the statement jinja will eat all the whitespace, newline and spaces. If you add a leading ""-"" to the statement then jinja will eat all the leading whitespace and newlines. All options ruin whitespace formatting and you're forced to putting the Jinja2 templates inline--making reading the source material more challenging.",{},{}

* The attacker should then use a Unix/linux client to load it: **this is the step I want to avoid as I'd like to  load it from Windows.**

Friendly greetings from over there at `numpy-groupies`. Looks like we're doing quite similar stuff there. What do you guys think of joining forces and making one package out of it, instead of two? `numpy-groupies` already had such a join a while ago, and collaboration works quite well so far. You did a good job in documenting the package and also have a bunch of features we didn't implement yet. At the same time, we'd have to offer drastic speedups for many functions. `np.add.at` and other ufuncs are sadly slow, and also pre-sorting arrays is a tedious task, that can be omitted in many cases. Pls check out our project, and let me know what you think!

6649,IssuesEvent,2017-08-30 16:30:44+00:00,59868262,aws/chalice,9696606,johntmyers,2232217,aws,opened,raw_body property inconsistency,[],[],0,"When no body is sent, and `app.current_request.raw_body` is used, there is inconsistent behavior between local Chalice testing and actually how it runs in Lambda.
For local testing, if no body is sent `self._body` is getting set to a string: `{}`

However, when no body is provided in an actual call to API gateway, `self._body` is `None` and there is an unhandled AttributeError exception when this executes: `self._raw_body = self._body.encode('utf-8')`
This effectively makes it hard for an app developer to gracefully handle a missing body from a call and will always yield a 500 error back to the user.

I personally see no reason to catch if body is None within `local.py` this effectively gives a false impression of how a request will be handled by Lambda itself. Instead the None type should be handled within the `raw_body` property IMO.
def create_lambda_event(self, method, path, headers, body=None):
# type: (str, str, Dict[str, str], str) -> EventType

6811,IssuesEvent,2015-12-21 19:27:12+00:00,37273087,TACC/agavepy,630606,mrhanlon,840408,TACC,opened,Using AgavePy to refresh a token maintained outside of AgavePy,[],[],0,"This issue is migrated from [Bitbucket](https://bitbucket.org/taccaci/agavepy/issues/14).

When using AgavePy as a library within a larger application (e.g., Django), we persist the user's OAuth token outside of the Agave object, e.g., in the session or in the database. It is useful, then, to be able to test and refresh the token directly without needing to make an additional API call. AgavePy will refresh the token automatically when an API call fails due to expired token, but this then results in two API calls, the response of which we don't need at all.

In Django we have an application/authentication backend that walks the authorization code flow to get a token and then calls the /profiles API to validate the token and get the user profile to determine the user's identity. Upon success we persist the token in the session. Later, we need to refresh the token when it expires. This is relatively easy to do using requests, but the preference would be to be able to instantiate an instance of `agavepy.agave.Token` and call `token.refresh()` directly to retrieve a new token.

Here is what we are doing now. When we get a token (at login) we record with it the time of retrieval. Later, in a Django middleware class, we hook `process_request` to 1) to see if the token is still valid and if not, 2) refresh the token:
A stub class to mimic the Agave object that is typically the ""parent"" of

We end up having to mock the `agavepy.agave.Agave` class, which `Token` expects to be it's `parent` attribute. We also need to manually set the `token_info` after instantiating the Token because there isn't a way to pass the `refresh_token` in the constructor. This works fine, but it is certainly not ideal.
It would be great if we could instantiate a Token efficiently and independently of the rest of AgavePy.",{},{}

6953,IssuesEvent,2015-10-12 20:43:42+00:00,6670942,boto/botocore,190930,mtdowling,327752,boto,closed,Cannot add x-amz-server-side-encryption header to S3 upload part,[],[],4,"I have an S3 bucket with a policy restricting to only KMS encryption (similar to http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingServerSideEncryption.html but with 'aws:kms'). I am attempting to do a multipart upload as below:
This fails, apparently because the upload part request does not include an x-amz-server-side-encryption header.
botocore.exceptions.ClientError: An error occurred (AccessDenied) when calling the UploadPart operation: Access Denied

Unlike PutObjectRequest, UploadPartRequest appears not to have a ServerSideEncryption member to set the x-amz-server-side-encryption header. This header is documented at http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/mpUploadUploadPart.html (this documentation appears incomplete since aws:kms is not listed as a valid value).
Without the bucket policy, the code sample executes successfully (and the resulting object is encrypted); but this no longer enforces that all uploads use encryption.

The boto3 documentation at https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html also states that SSECustomerAlgorithm can be set to aws:kms, but AFAICT that only sets the header for non-KMS SSE; the documentation appears to be faulty in listing aws:kms as an example value.",{},{}

6997,IssuesEvent,2017-11-05 15:05:55+00:00,30509483,clab/dynet,263363,danielhers,2374376,clab,closed,Precompiled wheel,"[{'id': 175078387, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/clab/dynet/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}]",[],14,"Installation of the `master` branch with pip is currently supported by `pip install git+https://github.com/clab/dynet#egg=dynet`.

To support installation by a simple `pip install dynet` (and versioning!), we need to upload a binary distribution package to PyPI. I think it would be a good idea to upload a precompiled .whl file, to reduce installation time significantly. It must be a separate file for Linux, OSX and Windows, compiled on the respective systems. I think this should be done once there is a 2.0 release.

However, I'm pretty sure it won't work with the current configuration where there is a main `setup.py` calling the `python/setup.py` which is generated from `setup.py.in`, because this way the main one doesn't know about the Cython extensions, and they are missing from the created wheel. Therefore, I believe the two `setup.py` files should be combined into a single static one, maybe replacing the CMake `${...}` directives by environment variables or parameters.",{},{}

However, I came across a weird issue, first when trying to add a new node to my GCE cluster (was just following the documentation here http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/add_remove_host/)

[error] server.go:110 HTTP Error: statusCode=500 open /var/lib/docker/repositories-btrfs: no such file or directory
Then, I realised that if you shut the docker daemon down it does not start up again:

Which I was able to narrow down to...

So, it looks like docker does not like something. I was able to find something similar issue in docker https://github.com/docker/docker/issues/7585 that is somehow related to mounted partitions, however that didn't help me much as I am not mounting anything myself.

One more thing: if I restart the node - docker daemon does start on boot. The other concerning thing I figured out was - if I take an existing node in the cluster and restart it - I get exactly the same problem. So I am not only not able to spin up additional nodes, but I am also potentially capable of losing the working ones...
After some more playing around with it I found a **workaround** (hope that might shed some light?).

I modified the docker.service file to specify the working directory different from /var/lib/docker (the best one I was able to find ended up being /var/lib/docker/docker, so that it shares the same partition)
and re-enabled the docker service. After that all the other services managed to get successfully restarted within minutes. ...

Symlink libffi includes to gcc default include path:
While it works it's an unsatisfying solution.

We could update current [disable-pkg-config.patch](https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/blob/0.5.3/pythonforandroid/recipes/cffi/disable-pkg-config.patch) to `try` to run, but `except` silently. That way if `dpkg-config` is available on the host, we can use it, but it won't fail if not.
Since `cffi` is not the first package, nor the last needing `dpkg-config`, we could create a recipe for it to make it available for the target architecture.
I'm not too sure about that one, but the idea would be to update the `libffi` recipe so it also makes the library/headers available for the host so we could point to it. But it could be a mess.

I have a preference for the ""`dpkg-config` when possible (i.e. host)"" solution. It seems easy enough to implement and it would fix the issue. For the long run I prefer if we had `dpkg-config` available for the target, but I'm not sure how achievable it is.
I would be glad to contribute more to python-for-android, so please let me know which solution you prefer and I could work on a pull request when I get time.",{},{}

7363,IssuesEvent,2018-02-23 18:32:48+00:00,79510167,pypa/pipenv,119893,kennethreitz,647025,pypa,closed,Prompt the user before creating a virtualenv,[],[],3,Be sure to check the existing issues, both open and closed.

If you are in a directory not associated with a virtualenv, `pipenv install` will create it. While it's a great feature, I noticed during numerous training I gave that it bites the beginners more often than not:
- they often pipenv install from the wrong directory by mistake
- they will install the wrong python version
I'm suggesting that if pipenv is run in a directory not associated with a virtualenv, then it should not immediatly proceed. It should pause, and prompt the user with something like:
But this directory is not associated with a virtualenv yet. What do you want to do:
[1] Create a virtualenv with Python x.w
[2] Create a virtualenv with Python x.x
[3] Create a virtualenv with Python y.y
[4] Create a virtualenv with Python y.x
- Limit creation by mistake in the wrong directory.
- Allow the user to know that there are several Python executables available and that he/she can choose.
Of course, this prompt should be disabled with an option such as --no-prompt for automation.

,{},{}

7775,IssuesEvent,2015-04-02 21:54:56+00:00,4127088,Azure/azure-sdk-for-python,4700946,Segsfault,6844498,Azure,opened,Low BLOB_MAX_CHUNK_DATA_SIZE leads to performance hits for max_connections=1,[],[],0,"When using azure.storage.blobservice.get_blob_to_file, the algorithm will download a 64MB (or smaller) file in one call REST call.

However larger files are chunked in 4MB blocks.  For a 65MB file this would result in 17 calls, which significantly decreases performance.  For my application, changing _BLOB_MAX_CHUNK_DATA_SIZE to be the same as _BLOB_MAX_DATA_SIZE (64MB) improved performance drastically.
Not sure of all use cases to know if this will impact other applications, but if it cannot be hard coded to 64MB, perhaps allowing a chunk_size  argument would help.",{},{}

7920,IssuesEvent,2018-02-09 15:24:06+00:00,31315021,Kinto/kinto,17006,glasserc,13413813,Kinto,closed,Is it possible to reduce write load on create() without reintroducing TOCTOU bugs?,[],[],3,"In #1406, we changed the create() method from doing ""read, then insert"" to doing ""insert with on conflict"". This introduced a lot of write load. Unlike read load, which can often be served using CPU and memory, write load always goes to disk and is therefore more expensive. This manifested by a dramatic increase on write load in the kintowe stack, because every request is creating a default bucket and a collection in that default bucket.

Is it possible to reduce write load without the risk of TOCTOU bugs? Now that we are fairly confident in the correctness of our `INSERT` + `ON CONFLICT` strategy, we could try to re-add the ""read, then insert"" strategy. The read would be optimistic -- if it's there, then we don't have to insert, but if it isn't there, we can insert and maybe get a conflict, no big deal. The risk here is that we could find that the row was there, and then fail to create it even though it's been deleted just afterwards by a different transaction. However, this may be acceptable, because child elements don't actually rely on the presence or absence of their ancestor records, and even if they do, such behavior is no different from trying the INSERT + ON CONFLICT and then having another thread delete the record just afterwards.

8208,IssuesEvent,2017-05-10 10:05:03+00:00,6235174,conda/conda,1418419,kalefranz,6392739,conda,closed,Conda metapackages should support channel-specs when specifying dependencies,[],[],3,All conda packages (including meta-packages) should have some mechanism for specifying where dependencies come from in it's meta-data.   In other words, the author of the package should be able to require that conda install the dependency from that channel.
Suppose we are installing package ""foo"" which has a specified dependency ""bar"" from channel ""doge""
Scenario 1:  a suitable ""bar"" is already installed (satisfies version)
conda should check to see if the package was installed already from channel ""doge"" if so, it proceeds.  If not, then force install ""bar"" again from channel ""doge"" (See Scenario 3)
Scenario 2:  a suitable ""bar"" is not installed and a suitable ""bar"" is not found in channel ""doge""
Scenario 3:  a suitable ""bar"" is not installed and a suitable ""bar"" is found in channel ""doge""
\* install any dependencies from the merged channel index and ""doge""

8233,IssuesEvent,2018-03-03 03:53:31+00:00,6235174,conda/conda,823355,cfobel,6392739,conda,opened,feat(noarch): add support for installing noarch header packages,[],[],0,"As discussed ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues#440 (see [here](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/440#issuecomment-370073253) and [here](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/440#issuecomment-370072215)), it would be helpful handle installing `noarch` packages containing only C/C++ headers.  Currently, this is not possible, since the Conda `include` path differs based on architecture.
For example, on Windows, the path is:

As suggested by @msarahan , one possibility could be to add an option to `conda build` recipes something like `noarch: includes` (see conda/conda-build#2725).  This would also require changes to `conda` to add special handling when installing such packages to install to the appropriate include path based on the platform of the Conda install environment.

My suggestion would be to either extract the src attribute if the whole iframe code is pasted into the embed field, or even better, to accept the URL of the video (in the format https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XXXXXXXXXXX) and convert that to an embed link.
If I remember correctly, Django Embed Video uses the last option, which IMHO greatly enhances usability.",{},{}

9274,IssuesEvent,2017-06-14 14:39:17+00:00,48501841,django/daphne,128536,sjamaan,27804,django,opened,Unbounded memory consumption with redis asgi,[],[],0,"We've noticed our server running out of memory due to concurrent incoming requests outstripping Daphne's ability to keep up with response writing. This is probably related to issue #65.
This can be tested relatively easily by initializing a new Django project and then replace `urls.py` with this example:
Then spin up redis, daphne and one worker, and hit it with for example [siege](https://www.joedog.org/siege-home/):
Now, watch `redis-server` eat up more and more memory. In some cases (but not all?), `daphne` also starts eating up more and more memory.
This is with 100 concurrent clients. Either adding more clients or increasing the response size make the memory use grow faster.
I don't really understand what is going wrong here. It looks like the workers are producing data faster than Daphne is able to respond.

Of course, this is a very synthetic test. In reality I use `django.http.FileResponse` to stream a file of 30MB or so to tens of clients and this is causing both Daphne and Redis to use 1GB of memory before the OOM killer gets invoked.",{},{}

9357,IssuesEvent,2018-02-21 01:44:43+00:00,26784827,dotnet/core,8228359,Petermarcu,9141961,dotnet,closed,dotnet core application aborts without exceptions,[],[],0,"# Issue Title
.net core 2 console application stops execution and quits without providing any kind of exception messages, except short notice ""**Aborted**""
The issue is only reproducible on Linux, but not Windows.
- compiled on Windows 10 x64 with command: **dotnet publish -c release -r debian-x64**
During the deserialization (or initialization) of objects of a certain type - application crashes on Linux environment immediately.
A sample code for issue reproduction is:
// if real library is used then Symbol.BNB_BTC or (Symbol)""BNBBTC"" will do
The `Symbol` class is implemented with a type-safe Enum pattern:
public static readonly Symbol ONE = new Symbol(new[] { OrderType.Limit, OrderType.LimitMaker, OrderType.Market, OrderType.StopLossLimit, OrderType.TakeProfitLimit });
public static readonly Symbol TWO = new Symbol(new[] { OrderType.Limit, OrderType.LimitMaker, OrderType.Market, OrderType.StopLossLimit, OrderType.TakeProfitLimit });
public static readonly Symbol THREE = new Symbol(new[] { OrderType.Limit, OrderType.LimitMaker, OrderType.Market, OrderType.StopLossLimit, OrderType.TakeProfitLimit });
In the example above, upon initialization of `Symbol` type object - application would abort at rate 1/5 (or more often/rarely):
> while sleep 0.5 ; do dotnet ConsoleApp1.dll ; done
The linux command will execute dotnet application once in half-a-second time. Therefore, if at least one ""Aborted"" is printed within 20 second interval - you can consider the test as not passed.
I was able to come up with a temporary solution which seems to be working at the moment.
If we change an anonymous type of object in constructor parameter list to `List` - then that will work nicely.
`new List<OrderType> { OrderType.Limit, OrderType.LimitMaker, OrderType.Market, OrderType.StopLossLimit, OrderType.TakeProfitLimit }`
Also, it was confirmed, that `OrderType[]` type of object yields absolutely the same result as `new[]`

Also, it was confirmed (on my machine), that if the number of static properties is halved, then the issue is gone, therefore, for better effect - test the solution when more than 200 `Symbol`s are defined in `Symbol` class.
The easiest steps to reproduce the issue is to:
1. Download the same library as I did [here](https://github.com/sonvister/Binance).
2. Change the source for the `Symbol` class, so that anonymous object is used (author recently applied a fix, per my request)
3. add new console application (see description section)
4. compile and then run on Linux (see testing section)
If you see the ""Aborted"" message after some time, then that's the issue.
1. see some kind of exception message when such errors are thrown
Rest is up to you. Good luck.",{},{}

9434,IssuesEvent,2015-04-14 19:43:26+00:00,2494665,fluent/fluent-logger-python,7683836,mr-salty,859518,fluent,opened,logging handler yields 'record' as string instead of map,[],[],0,"When using the logging handler, the emitted record has type string (fixedstr) rather than map.

According to the fluent plugin docs at http://docs.fluentd.org/articles/plugin-development 'record' is required to be a Hash object, so I believe the implication is this module should only emit 'map'.
Example code (based on the examples from the docs), followed by some extra logging I added to fluentd's in_forward plugin:
2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: on_message:  msg=[""app.follow"", 1428704107, ""{'message': 'test from info'}""] chunk_size=47 source=""host: 127.0.0.1, addr: 127.0.0.1, port: 49307""
Example using 'event' directly, which works correctly:
2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: on_message:  msg=[""app.follow"", 1428704107, {""message""=>""this is a test""}] chunk_size=41 source=""host: 127.0.0.1, addr: 127.0.0.1, port: 49306""
Some output plugins do not care about the type of 'record' so this may appear to work, but there are other plugins that discard the record or have other issues if it is not a hash.",{},{}

Read here for beginners FAQ,[],"[{'login': 'christopherhesse', 'id': 440336, 'node_id': 'MDQ6VXNlcjQ0MDMzNg==', 'avatar_url': 'https://avatars2.githubusercontent.com/u/440336?v=4', 'gravatar_id': '', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse', 'html_url': 'https://github.com/christopherhesse', 'followers_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/followers', 'following_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/following{/other_user}', 'gists_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/gists{/gist_id}', 'starred_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/starred{/owner}{/repo}', 'subscriptions_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/subscriptions', 'organizations_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/orgs', 'repos_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/repos', 'events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/events{/privacy}', 'received_events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/christopherhesse/received_events', 'type': 'User', 'site_admin': False}]",2,"Noticed that the official contest support is severely understaffed and thus unresponsive when it comes to technical support, so I've compiled a couple pointers to help people get started. I will update this as I progress. Feel free to add any common issues and resolutions that you have noticed as well.
### Q1. Do I need to pay for the sonic games? ###
A1. Yes, using other ROMs not from steam have not worked for me personally.
### Q2. Can't install sonic on my mac/linux ###
A2. I got them by using Windows, another contestant mentioned using wine with linux; Installing Steam and copying the roms from there.
### Q3. Script ```python -m retro.import.sega_classics``` to import ROMs doesn't work ###
A3. Try moving ROMs from your steam folder to your working folder, then ```python -m retro.import .```
### Q4. Docker doesn't install for Windows because you don't have Pro. ###

### Q5. CPU-Tensorflow gives me a warning message: ```tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use ... instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.``` ###
A5. Don't fear, this isn't too hard to resolve. Give this a read: https://gist.github.com/venik/9ba962c8b301b0e21f99884cbd35082f and here https://www.tensorflow.org/install/install_sources
### Q6. Don't have GPU and getting ```ImportError: libcuda.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory```
A6. You need to use docker to build two different images, one for testing locally and one for submitting. Will add details after I figure out how to do this...
### Q7. Can I submit a job through Docker CLI instead of the contest webpage ###

```retro-contest login --server https://contest.openai.com  && retro-contest job submit -t $TAG_NAME ``` will automatically tag $TAG_NAME as $REGISTRY_NAME/$TAG_NAME, push, and start the job note that it pulls down $REGISTRY_NAME for you so it doesn't need to be set locally",{},{}

9846,IssuesEvent,2017-08-25 14:44:03+00:00,38581626,pybind/pybind11,9640520,rleigh-codelibre,17565521,pybind,opened,Segfault with map_caster and GCC 4.8,[],[],0,Make sure you've completed the following steps before submitting your issue -- thank you!

When wrapping a shared library which contains methods returning a reference to a map, the wrapper segfaults when trying to use the map.  This occurs with GCC 4.8.5 on CentOS7 and is independent of the Python version used.  If I use GCC 5.3.1 from devtoolset-4, it works just fine.  I've tried with Python 2.6.5, 2.7.13 and 3.5.1, and it works in all cases with GCC 5.3.1 and fails in all cases with GCC 4.8.5.
It looks like the fault is in map_caster (see the segfault below).
I have tested building the shared library and pybind11 wrapper with different GCC versions:

It's quite likely there's something odd happening specifically with GCC 4.8, but I'm unsure if pybind11 is also doing something to trigger this condition, which might make it possible to work around the problem.  Or it might be a subtle issue inside map_caster.

I've created a small reduced testcase here: https://github.com/rleigh-codelibre/test-pybind11 comprising a small shared library with the method returning the map, plus the pybind11 wrapper and unit test.  The steps to build and test are show below, along with the output I get in a plain CentOS 7 VM.

9851,IssuesEvent,2016-10-24 23:56:16+00:00,38581626,pybind/pybind11,2418513,aldanor,17565521,pybind,closed,Type casters for NumPy scalars,[],[],9,"It would be nice to be able to pass structured NumPy scalars back to C++, e.g.:
struct A { int a; float b; A() = default; A(int a) : a(a), b(a + 1) {} };
It would be great to have this work:
(it already works with arithmetic NumPy types, but mostly because they inherit from the builtins)

Because structured types are always POD, they're default constructible and don't have a user destructor; so basically all we need to do is a reinterpret cast. Each element  of the structured array is of type `np.void`:

10011,IssuesEvent,2018-05-08 15:40:14+00:00,51625787,vatlab/SoS,9889312,BoPeng,20074389,vatlab,opened,task management under environments with shared home directories,[],[],0,"A pretty typical academic computing environment has a number of servers with shared home directories. The `/home/user` directory is automatically mounted so that users will see the same home directory on all servers they log in. The servers themselves can have different capacities and software so that users can login to particular servers for particular purposes.
Problems, however, might arise for the management of tasks. For example,
1. What if two servers try to run the same tasks? Because of the use of lock files, one of the servers will have to wait but the reason for wait might not be clear to the user.
2. `sos purge --all` will clear all tasks, regardless of servers.
3. A remote task can be killed locally. This is not necessarily a bad thing but could be confusing.",{},{}

10402,IssuesEvent,2018-04-27 23:01:14+00:00,91356408,allenai/allennlp,3291951,matt-gardner,5667695,allenai,opened,Use inspection on __init__ for from_params,[],[],0,"I just ran into a bug where I had changed the default value in `__init__` for a particular argument, but forgot to change it in `from_params`.  It's unfortunate we need to repeat ourselves in `from_params` so much, and I wonder if there's a way we could make this simpler using `inspect`.  Like, get the type annotation of the argument, use that to know how to do `params.pop`, pass the default value from the inspection along to the `params.pop`, etc.  I imagine you could just have a default implementation on `Registrable` that just does inspection on the constructor object, and have it just work for almost all classes. 10503,IssuesEvent,2015-12-17 02:51:17+00:00,43388350,awslabs/aws-shell,368057,jamesls,3299148,awslabs,closed,Add toolbar menu to easily toggle between options,[],[],3,"# Toolbar Options
* `F4` toggles single and multiple columns in the autocompletion menu
Some users might want to turn off fuzzy matching.  I sometimes toggle this depending on what I'm doing.
On a related note, I think the current fuzzy matching works pretty well, although in some cases it actually results in more keystrokes (https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/issues/5).  For example:

Currently `aws-shell` runs with `Vi`bindings, which might not suit everyone.  For `SAWS` and `aws-shell` personally I'm starting to use the `Emacs` mode more it feels more natural to me thinking of these apps as a command line tool rather than an editor.
TODO: With `Vi` bindings we might want to add info to let the user know what mode is active.
## Single vs Multi Column Completion Menus

Some users might prefer the current multi column and others might prefer the single column.  I prefer the latter it seems 'less busy' and more like what I'm accustomed to with other apps, although it could take some scrolling unlike the multi column option where you see pretty much everything.

`prompt-toolkit` also handles the `meta` info differently single vs multi.  For single you see more `meta` right away, for multi, you only see `meta` for the currently highlighted completion.  I've also applied `pygments` styles to the menu and `meta`, although the multi `meta` style doesn't seem to be taking hold.

The help pane is really cool, although it does take up a lot of space.  Users might not always want to see this menu--for example they might only want to see this for unfamiliar commands.  Users  might want to reclaim that screen real estate.
To exit, `Ctrl-D` might not be obvious to some users.
These options should probably go to a config file to save their state between sessions.
We could also make the toolbar content configurable if the number of options starts growing large.

,{},{}

10770,IssuesEvent,2017-10-18 17:13:27+00:00,12235583,elastic/curator,6018053,dandrestor,6764390,elastic,opened,"During shrink action, do not wait for other shards",[],[],0,"During the execution of a shrink action, I identified two stages at which curator will wait for shard recovery to complete:
* Moving shards to shrink node: xxx

I suspect at these stages, curator does a GET _cluster/health?wait_for_no_relocating_shards or something similar. This makes curator to wait even for other recoveries, for indexes other than the index currently being shrunk. Curator should take a closer look at recovery and only wait for the current shards to recover.",{},{}

10815,IssuesEvent,2017-02-22 09:12:04+00:00,9801644,elastic/elasticsearch-py,32132,HonzaKral,6764390,elastic,closed,Using a GET request with a request body.,[],[],1,"It appears that the elastic search client uses a GET request with a body for the query DSL endpoint. I just discovered an issue with Google App Engine's HTTP fetching were the GET body is stripped. To my knowledge, the HTTP standard does not specify what should happen with a the body of a GET request provided it does not change the semantics of the request. The GET method makes sense since the query is an idempotent, (mostly) cache-able read request.
Perhaps it would be better funnel the query DSL json through an alternative method or a parameter on the GET request? This would reduce the possibility of intermediary proxies doing strange things.
I filed a bug with Google to have them fix it on their end, but the client should not use non-standard functionality.

This issue has been there since day one. The socket library I'm using is https://github.com/Zewo/ZeroMQ.git.

I run notebook using `jupyter-notebook --Session.key=""b''"" --Session.debug=True --debug`. According to the logs printed, the first `kernel_info_request` was received by my kernel and the `kernel_info_reply` is sent successfully (based on the return value of the zmq's `zmq_send` method) by my kernel as well. However, it's still timeout on Notebook end.

I briefly looked through the implementation related to this and found [this](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/services/kernels/handlers.py#L234). The code looks okay to me so I'm confused.

I know this might be an issue on my end, but I'm sure the `kernel_info_reply` message has been sent successfully (at least according to the zmq's return value.) I wonder if you guys have seen such issue before?
BTW, the kernel i'm writing is also open source at [here](https://github.com/KelvinJin/iSwift.Linux) but you probably don't want to look at it...
One thing to note is that after the timeout, the session continues and somehow sends another `kernel_info_request`. This time the `kernel_info_reply` is received successfully.
Also, the first `kernel_info_request` and the subsequent one are different in terms of the `msg_id` and `username`.
Can we expect Variable inspector (like the one in spyder IDE) in the near future

11055,IssuesEvent,2017-07-30 01:35:23+00:00,525316,mozilla/bleach,204779,jvanasco,131524,mozilla,opened,escaping of URLs can causes issues in fragile context scenarios,[],[],0,_This issue is forked from discussion in #143 and #293_

Problems can arise from the plaintext escaping of URLs by the parser and how it relates to `linkify`. This is magnified when `linkify` is applied to `clean` text, but can also have issues when dealing with ""replied"" content.
There are two variants of this issue, which concerns how the parser escapes the `&` character in a url into the HTML entity `&` sequence of characters:
* cases that involve a ""fragile syntax"" (using the W3C's terms)

First, I apologize to @willkg for wasting your time on the `&` encoding.  I was going by RFCs on HTTP and didn't dive into the HTML specs, which define that a browser should decode the `&` and will request the ""intended"" url.  modern gui browsers all do this; commandline browsers seem hit-or-miss in catching this.  sending the html encoded url may or may-not work, as both `&` and `;` are often configured as the default delimiters (w3c and large projects seem to be standardizing on `&` only).  My bad.
This is the w3c example in which `&` must be pre-escaped, since `©` is a named character reference.  the link `?art&copy` will render as `art©`
Art and Copy
The existing code and current candidate are both deficient in handling this.
And the results on `v1.4.3` and the current #293

,{},{}

11359,IssuesEvent,2018-08-26 03:52:50+00:00,96454557,nteract/papermill,1863892,MSeal,12401040,nteract,opened,Better record capabilities,"[{'id': 1014073565, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMDE0MDczNTY1', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/nteract/papermill/labels/idea', 'name': 'idea', 'color': 'a80631', 'default': False}]",[],0,"Challenges with record today:

- Doesn't _quite_ fit with the rest of papermill linear execution codebase (it was awkward to describe it as an additional feature of papermill this week, really felt like describing a second less developed library)
- Requires translation to JSON for everything (dataframes are painful here)
- Reading records into a result dataframe isn't always the shape you'd expect
- Not as modular as other components of papermill where we can register custom operators (hard to do cross notebook?)
I was thinking perhaps we should promote notebook data recording to a new repository in nteract (@betatim suggested some names -- `scrapbook.glue(stuff)`)
A few ideas regardless of where it lives, on the topic:
- Use arrow for dataframe like saves?
- Some other binary forms (plug and play options?)
- Support other standard storage forms as plug and play options (pandas does this -- i.e. steal pandas conversion options as plugins as they're nice features)
- Maybe lean on some of the other data transport ideas between kernels and components from elsewhere nteract??
- Think a little more about secondar language support (R is nice but we'd want support in many languages for the most common transports)

11438,IssuesEvent,2017-05-11 14:44:41+00:00,16366081,numenta/nupic.core,364113,mrcslws,1039191,numenta,opened,"On py::Bool, the PyObject* constructor shouldn't do an INCREF",[],[],0,"The original change in https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/pull/1295 was good, but then I made an incorrect change before merging: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/pull/1295/commits/2a432cdcf8f2e5fa02b07a4f5fd2253f214ede2e
Now when you call `getParameter` with a bool, it increments the reference count.

When constructing from a PyObject*, these PyHelpers classes assume that the PyObject already has already had an INCREF. It gives callers a way to not have to deal with refcounts, for example here: https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/1bcc78f28892582dd9fb6cf19eda16e21c31ec54/src/nupic/regions/PyRegion.cpp#L767",{},{}

'received_events_url': 'https://api.github.com/users/pnuu/received_events', 'type': 'User', 'site_admin': False}]",0,"#### Problem description

Currently the enhancement configs and default enhancer use trollimage to scale the data to a 0-1 range. The writer can then use `img._finalize(data_type)` to scale the image back to a 0-255 (or other data type) range. This performs more operations than may be necessary, but does provide a nice interface where an Image object can be assumed to be normalized. This gets a little strange when dealing with float data types since they typically aren't stretch and therefore aren't normalized in the Image object.

I'm creating this issue to start the discussion: Is normalizing to 0-1 then back to the data type range the best option? Is it a waste of processing? How would not pre-normalizing be any less wasteful? Is there a benefit to the stretching knowing the output data type (rounding differently, etc)? Is there a loss of precision by normalizing and then un-normalizing?

Lastly, I bring up my usual point, is trollimage as used by satpy worth having as a separate package? Does any other package use it? Will any other package *ever* use it? It currently consists of 3 modules with the largest being the Image class and its conversion utilities. Much of the code for the Image class is for different types of stretching. Would it be better for this logic to be absorbed by SatPy? If someone wants to add a new type of enhancement to satpy long term (square root for example) then the proper way right now would be to add it to trollimage, right? That seems a little annoying to maintain.
One less package to maintain versus possible future flexibility.",{},{}

11806,IssuesEvent,2017-11-03 16:46:31+00:00,26827282,tilezen/mapbox-vector-tile,271360,zerebubuth,19462720,tilezen,opened,Polygon validity and performance,[],[],0,"Making sure that polygons are valid (which we're not even doing 100% at, see #95 & #87) takes up, on average, about 60% of the time it takes to format an MVT tile. Formatting MVT tiles takes up around 21% of total runtime, and formatting tiles in general takes around 50% of all runtime.

We know that polygon validity is hard, but we need it for V2 #42. We also know that performance could be better #72. Currently, we recurse trying to make the polygon valid #76, which can account for a lot of time for polygons which flip-flop between being valid with fractional coordinates and invalid when we round them to integers.

There are other bits of software we could use to do the polygon validity checking, e.g: [prepair](https://github.com/tudelft3d/prepair) or [CGAL](http://www.cgal.org), or we could do a better job of [ensuring polygon validity while rebuilding](https://github.com/tilezen/mapbox-vector-tile/blob/879569ac994acb1684e8690cc94f33c862c4459c/mapbox_vector_tile/polygon.py#L127-L153) (while avoiding [known issues](https://trac.osgeo.org/geos/ticket/789)). A neat method that someone else invented, but I've been unable to find a source for, would be to render the polygon at the integer resolution we want for MVT, then run [marching squares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marching_squares) to recover a polygon.

11924,IssuesEvent,2016-09-15 10:14:17+00:00,38744670,zalando/patroni,3407345,CyberDem0n,1564818,zalando,closed,Support bootstrapping spilo cluster from the external PostgreSQL database,"[{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zalando/patroni/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb'}]",[],5,"There is a valid use case of moving already running databases to spilo without doing an expensive dump/restore. In order to support it, we can just pretend that an external PostgreSQL data service is part of the spilo cluster, acting as a leader, with TTL that never expires. Once the cluster is started, spilo nodes will choose replica roles (as the leader is already taken) and will continue to act as replicas until the leader key is explicitly removed by spilo (since it cannot expire on its own).
In order to support this use case, the following parameters should be changed:
- a new primary_conninfo parameter should be added to the yaml configuration for Patroni.

- a global 'standby_mode' flag should be adedd to the DCS at /service/cluster_name/, inidicating that the cluster is containing a non-Patroni managed leader, while all the spilo nodes are running as replicas. This flag would change some operations, i.e. replication slots creation/removal.
From the user point-of-view, the spilo cluster should be configured with the primary_conninfo, pointing to the external PostgreSQL database.

This connection string should also allow normal (non-replication) superuser connections, and the pg_hba.conf on the cluster should be configured to permit such connections from the addresses that would belong to the spilo cluster.
The changes required in the spilo workflow are the following:
- When the first patroni node joins the new (non-initialized cluster), it would set the following keys right after acquiring the initialize one:

column2: - /service/cluster_name/member/external_$random_suffix with the connection string to the external service (primary_conninfo parameter)
- /service/cluster_name/leader/optime with the value 0 (since the leader is not supposed to update the key, but it's still required for some code inside Patroni)
- /service/cluster_name/stanby_mode to signal that the cluster is running in a special mode.

column1: - When the other spilo nodes join the DCS and observe the standby_mode flag, they start sleeping, periodically checking until this flag is gone. This would prevent cloning an external source multiple times.
- The active patroni will connect to the master and create a new replication slot. Afterwards, it will clone the data from the master and will run the streaming replication.

- At the point the DBA decides the cluster should be separated from the external PostgreSQL node, it would remove the leader key and the member entry, corresponding to that key, also cleaning the replication slot created in the external PostgreSQL node. At this point, the active spilo cluster will come up, taking the leader role (unconditionally, by observing the standby_mode flag) and remove the standby_mode flag, so that other spilo members can start building itself from the leader. Note that the attempt to remove that key before the active spilo replica becomes available should be ignored.

That command should be built into the spilo comamand-line client, as it's not possible to find the promotion point automatically (for instance, we might stream current changes from the external master to spilo until a certain scheduled maintaince window, where the spilo cluster will be set as a master).
If the active spilo node dies while the standby_mode is still set, another node should notice it and take its place.

,{},{}

11943,IssuesEvent,2016-09-16 23:19:51+00:00,35882519,zalando/connexion,107613,rafaelcaricio,1564818,zalando,opened,Support for mocking API based on spec,"[{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zalando/connexion/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb'}]",[],0,"### Description

Recently we added support for applying a stub to unimplemented operations (https://github.com/zalando/connexion/pull/278). Following that line, I think would be interesting to add support for automatic mocking of a API based on the specification.
This mocking feature would work as the stub, only activated to not implemented operations. This is powerful because allows a broader use case for Connexion.
- Developers would get a better insight on how the API should look like after complete implementation;
- Frontend engineers could mock easily all APIs necessary to develop the client UI;
- Mobile and backend peers would be able to mock dependent services for testing;

12105,IssuesEvent,2017-05-28 19:36:19+00:00,32944298,Z3Prover/z3,549449,LeventErkok,11668813,Z3Prover,closed,SMT-Lib compliant printing of literals,[],[],4,"For the following input:
(:reason-unknown ""(error ""line 1 column 11: state of the most recent check-sat command is not known"")
This isn't SMTLib compliant, since the new standard speculates quote characters should be escaped by using two of them. So, it should look something like:
(:reason-unknown ""(error """"line 1 column 11: state of the most recent check-sat command is not known"")
Even then, it looks rather _bizarre_, perhaps this should simply print as:
(:reason-unknown ""line 1 column 11: state of the most recent check-sat command is not known"")
which I guess was the original intent but the call to `error` seems to be leaking in somehow.",{},{}

After reviewing some code within `middleware`, it looks like there is currently no dedicated protocol-enforcement available, most possibly because there was till now only one protocol (specs, yellow-paper) to support.

I have the (gut) feeling that a protocol-verifying code should reside within a separate middleware (like `specs`). The counter-argument is possibly that any further processing results in a compliancy-check. But still, a clean separation would make things cleaner.
Provide `middleware/compliancy/` or `middleware/specs/ `or `middleware/protocol/` (1), which contains the different middlewares which ensure compliancy to specs like:
Compliancy is loaded before pythonic / formatters. Data-fields are kept compatible to the relevant specification.
Compliancy should be 'natural', which means that if a user uses web3.py to connect to a chain, web3.py detects the chain-type, and uses the relevant compliancy-middleware.

If this concept hits on implementation-limits, then the protocol-handling can be implemented e.g. directly within a class ChainSpecs and descendants. But it seems that using middleware should be enough.
(1) **protocol**:  'protocol' is used in another context ( 'eth_protocolVersion': '63',). So, this would mean that this term cannot be used.

For the current resolution of #549 (and to avoid introducing to much code-changes) , it would be enough to provide a `/specs/poa_geth` or `/specs/eip225`, which would leave extraData(32) intact (**EXTRA_VANITY**), and move the rest of the data to a 2nd field (**EXTRA_SEAL**, 65 bytes), see ethereum/EIPs/issues/225

,{},{}

Looking at `openfisca-france`, it seems that most of the variables have a natural period granularity. For instance, the `rsa` is defined for a month, while `irpp` defined for a year.
The way this natural period is set for a given formula is today complex and confusing. For instance :
The current implementation allows a formula to return a result for a different period than the one it has been called for.

For instance, if I call `rsa` for `2015`, the first line`period = period.this_month` will change `period` to `2015-01`. This period will be returned with the result. It is then the openfisca middleware that will check that the returned period is the same than the required one. In our case, it will throw an error except if a certain option has been set for the calculation.
- Returning the period with the result is not natural, and often forgotten.
- The first line, which defines the natural period of the formula, is really unclear.
- The first line is repeated in almost all formulas.
If each variable has a natural period, then a **declarative approach** seems more simple.
period = MONTH # keyword to be discussed
- If I call a variable for a period that is **not its natural one**, **an error is raised** before entering the formula.
- For instance if I call `rsa` for `2015`, or `irpp` for `2015-01`.
- If a formula has no natural period, we do not check anything (some variables are period agnostics, for instance inputs or basis input combinations)",{},{}

- [ ] Cas type d'un célibataire sans enfants avec des charges déductibles non plafonnées

Last week I tried to have localstack running within my CI build in CircleCI and faced various problems. I got in touch with them and their engineers investigated the issue. Here's what they found out:
Looking at this particular container, it has such high uid that we will not be able to reasonably accomodate them while keeping user namespace effectively:
notnoop@placeholder:~$ sudo docker run -it --rm --entrypoint '' atlassianlabs/localstack:0.5.5 ls -lha /opt/code/localstack/localstack/dashboard/web/node_modules/comment-parser

The default limit is 65536 but README.md in this directory used 42864389 . This is probably as misconfiguration on the docker image creation part and we/user can consider contributing to image to avoid this.
Since a lot of people might use localstack within CircleCI, I figured it might be good to fix this to improve localstack adoption.

14327,IssuesEvent,2016-08-26 22:28:13+00:00,7209075,pantsbuild/pants,7973582,JieGhost,3065172,pantsbuild,closed,Clarify split and rename one/both of pants-ignore and ignore-patterns,[{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pantsbuild/pants/labels/engine', 'name': 'engine', 'color': 'fbca04'}]",[],1,"`--pants-ignore` is implemented by ProjectTree, and should be used when pants should always ignore a file: even in `globs` (example: `*.pyc` files, the `.git` directory, etc).

(ProjectTree is not yet used everywhere for filesystem consumption: most importantly, it is not used by twitter.commons, so it does not apply to the current implementation of globs. 14382,IssuesEvent,2016-11-30 11:11:40+00:00,30618733,prometheus/client_python,16303028,volkovandr,3380462,prometheus,opened,Multiprocessing works differently in Linux and Windows,[],[],0,"Hi
That's my small example script. Create a subdirectory ""metrics"" in the folder from you are running the script and set the environment variable.
This is just an example of how to use prometheus_client in multiprocessing application
If I run this in Windows I got this output:
and there are 10 files in the metrics subdirectory.
But on Linux the output is different:
And there is only one file in the directory.
I'm not quite sure but my guess is the following:
In the core.py at lines 441-444 the variable _ValueClass is initialized:
And then that class is instantiated and it creates the files where the metrics data will be stored.
The class itself is created in the function _MultiProcessValue() and it uses os.getpid() as a default value for the argument.

In Windows when you start another processes with _multiprocessing_ your main module is reloaded for each of the processes. And the prometheus_client library is also reloaded and the class is constructed for each of the new process.
In Lunux the module is loaded only once and therefore the class is created also only once.

All that makes the library write all the metrics of all the processes to the same file on Linux! That makes the metrics basically wrong, and in the worst case the file got corrupted and the HTTP server can not read from the file.
Though the stuff seems to work well if the load on the processes is small. E.g. if you put _sleep_ in the worker function and reduce the number of cycles it can (sometimes) work fine.
If i change the core.py in this way, it works fine on both Linux and Windows (lines 395-406):
'''A float protected by a mutex backed by a per-process mmaped file.'''
def __init__(self, typ, metric_name, name, labelnames, labelvalues, multiprocess_mode='', **kwargs):
but then the unit tests will most probably fail.
We need a test and fix for this asap!",{},{}

15450,IssuesEvent,2018-07-27 13:07:44+00:00,79083614,python-trio/trio,13807,miracle2k,26335827,python-trio,opened,Alternative supervision logic / custom nurseries,[],[],0,"I saw this topic mentioned a couple times in various tickets. Working with trio over the last couple of weeks, there were two specific kinds of behaviour that I felt I wanted to have.

One was a kind of ""main task"" mode. If the task ends, without an exception, I want the nursery to shutdown, rather than wait until other tasks are complete. This is a basically just a protection against ""this task should not really end without an exception, but due to some bug in does"". It's a pretty minor thing, so I won't talk about it more.

The more useful behaviour is a kind of ""daemon"" mode. A background task that will not block the nursery from exiting. I had of cases where I needed this; it was always about trying to process events concurrently with task.

My intention was to implement this in a custom nursery, and then ask here about what trio could do to make this kind of think easier or nicer. But I think the result turned out to be pretty nice and usable already, so maybe no action is required.
And I am using it like this:
Hopefully this is useful for some.

I know there was some talk about Erlang-style supervision trees; I am not sure what the current plan is regarding custom supervision logic, but from this experiment, it seems to me that working on top of the existing nursery API is pretty flexible.",{},{}
15451,IssuesEvent,2018-04-14 04:37:26+00:00,79083614,python-trio/trio,609896,njsmith,26335827,python-trio,opened,Python 3.8 wishlist,[],[],0,"3.7 is in beta now, and

* Try proposing a hook that runs on bare calls to async functions (so e.g. `await f()` doesn't call the hook, but `f()` does). Trio would make this an error (#79), asyncio would make it call `ensure_future`, twisted would call `asDeferred`, etc.
* There was a longish discussion of this [starting here](https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=5acf3b8927c509a774041463)

* Better MultiError support: the main two pain points are catching them, and printing them (in particular we can override `sys.excepthook`, but that doesn't help with other code that wants to print or capture tracebacks like IPython, pytest, raven, ...)

* Whatever we decide we need to improve handling of cancel scope/nursery interaction with generators. [PEP 568](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0568/) is one approach (#265). This is complicated because cancel scopes and nurseries have somewhat different needs. Or maybe we just want to disallow `yield`ing from inside them... but that would also need some kind of help from the interpreter.
* some way to make `@enable_ki_protection` atomic; for example, [bpo-12857](https://bugs.python.org/issue12857)",{},{}

In attempting this, I noticed link_df was running very slow.  After 12 hours, the code had completed under 8,000 frames (I estimate that the entire data set would have taken maybe 4 days).  While I understand that that is a huge data set, I previously was able to complete the linking of the same number of particles (~1,700) for 24,000 frames, which took about 4 hours.  Naively, I was expecting the time to scale linearly, and so this 90k frame data set would take 12-16 hours (much more reasonable than 4 days).  But this was not the case.

My hypothesis is there is a memory issue.  The 90k frame data file is ~3 GB, and uses 10-15% of my machine's memory (linux server, found using ""top"" command).  Any suggestions on how this process can be sped up?
Some useful information, versions of various packages are: python 2.7.10, numba 0.12.2, numpy 1.8.2, trackpy 0.2.4rc7, and pandas 0.14.1.

16052,IssuesEvent,2018-05-07 18:20:30+00:00,56217288,confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python,1752,bretthoerner,9439498,confluentinc,opened,"Survivng ""Commit failed: Broker: Specified group generation id is not valid"" on Broker restart",[],[],0,"Description
* Version 0.11.4 of both confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka.
My manually committing consumer is unable to survive a broker stopping. Maybe this is expected, but I'm not sure how people typically handle this use case:
* I poll until I have a desirable batch size to write to an external DB.
* I write the batch to the external DB.
* I call `consumer.commit(asynchronous=False)` to ensure the matching offsets are committed to the broker.
(Note that I write a batch (and commit to Kafka) at minimum every 10 seconds, and the flush to external DB happens in under 2 seconds. I don't think I'm running up against any timeouts.)

The issue is I'd like for my consumer to survive a broker restart, but I get the following when I stop one of my 3 brokers and I was in the middle of a DB write (which means a commit happens seconds later):
KafkaException: KafkaError{code=ILLEGAL_GENERATION,val=22,str=""Commit failed: Broker: Specified group generation id is not valid""}
What's more confusing, is that after that occurs it seems like the offsets in the broker are corrupted or removed, because when I start my consumer I get the following:
And then, of course, nothing happens. So I have to reset to earliest or latest (or manually find out the correct offsets).

* What's the proper way to survive `Commit failed: Broker: Specified group generation id is not valid`? In some of my research it seems like my only option is to continue to `poll()` and wait? But if so then how do I know when is safe? And by polling wouldn't I possibly be popping new messages off the queue and thus moving the offsets further than what I just wrote to my DB?
* On a `Commit failed: Broker: Specified group generation id is not valid` I would have expected nothing to be committed, but it seems to break all of my stored offsets? Is that expected?",{},{}

16068,IssuesEvent,2017-10-16 06:20:40+00:00,1750874,django-money/django-money,1236561,Stranger6667,12234694,django-money,closed,A model with multiple MoneyField sharing a single currency?,"[{'id': 643098338, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-money/django-money/labels/feature', 'name': 'feature', 'color': '91CA55', 'default': False}, {'id': 643098616, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-money/django-money/labels/in%20progress', 'name': 'in progress', 'color': 'FBCA04', 'default': False}]",[],3,"I have a model where there are multiple amounts, which must all be in a same single currency. I know I can just throw in multiple `MoneyFields` and ensure the consistency in `Model.clean`, but this would create unnecessarily many database fields, which I don't like. I could've also used plain and simple `DecimalField`s, but since I plan to use `Money` objects everywhere I want consistency in this regard (and don't want to construct those explicitly everywhere I need the field's value).
From what I understood from reading the source code, currently `MoneyField` always automatically creates paired `CurrencyField` and there's no control over this.
I wonder if a field type, or an extension of `MoneyField`'s API that'd use explicitly-created `CurrencyField` would be a good addition. I.e.:
In this scenario, to keep things simple and avoid unexpected bugs, assigning a value with a mismatching currency should probably result in an exception:
# This should fail and raise ValueError (or a subclass of it)
invoice = Invoice(currency=USD, total=Money(10, USD), paid=Money(0, EUR), ...)
invoice = Invoice(currency=USD, total=Money(10, USD), paid=Money(0, USD), ...)
invoice.paid = Money(5, USD)      # This is OK
invoice.paid = Money(8.79, EUR)   # This should fail

I had a limited success by subclassing and overriding `contribute_to_class` to return a specially prepared `MoneyFieldProxy` ([gist with my kludge that happens to work for me](https://gist.github.com/drdaeman/5d1a948e829ceb78af7afce701378545)), but it only works seemingly correctly if `CurrencyField` is properly listed above any dependent fields.
What do you think? Is something like this a good idea?

,{},{}

16085,IssuesEvent,2018-02-27 04:23:50+00:00,1151051,django-oscar/django-oscar,160227,solarissmoke,9057806,django-oscar,opened,Dashboard should not use Google Analytics by default,[],[],0,Currently all activity in the dashboard is sent to Google Analytics. I don't think this is appropriate for the following reasons:
- Many sites probably don't want to track dashboard activity at all anyway.

- Dashboard activity is completely different from front-end (end-user) activity and mixing them up results in skewed metrics for both (the bounce rate on the dashboard is almost 0 for example, but the conversion rate is also 0...).
- Even if you did want to track activity on the dashboard you should do so under a different analytics property so as not to conflate this with front-end activity.
On that basis I think we should override the `tracking` block in the dashboard templates by default.",{},{}

16887,IssuesEvent,2017-02-06 11:22:32+00:00,50646767,raiden-network/raiden,1658405,LefterisJP,19666847,raiden-network,closed,add getter methods to the RaidenAPI,"[{'id': 473091760, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/raiden-network/raiden/labels/low_hanging_fruit', 'name': 'low_hanging_fruit', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}]",[],1,"this is a split-up from #10, as stated in #325

This are mainly getter methods for a Client to retrieve the initial state of the node on the first connect. Ideally, every following state-update will be retrieved through events in a long-polling fashion, in order to keep the polling count low.
For now, the methods should only return internal Objects like e.g. a `raiden.channel.Channel`.

But in the end, we want the same Interface for external api-users (via TCP) and internal api-users (in-process). Therefore, the API-methods should return Objects that function as simple data-containers for all the relevant information in their decoded (binary) form.
Internal users of the API will simply process this objects, without worrying about encoding,
while external users will retrieve the JSON-encoded version of this objects.
This is a universal method for retrieving a collection of channels.

When neither of the keyword-arguments is provided, it should return all channel-objects that the node participates in. The keyword-arguments serve as a simple filter, so that the client can retrieve channels filtered by a certain asset- / partner address.
Returns only one channel-object, retrieved by the netting-channel-address.
This is not neccessarily needed, since we can retrieve all channels with `get_channel_list`,
but is a convienience method if the client is only interested in one channel, or needs to poll for state changes
Querys raiden's discovery to get the value of the `address - > endpoint` mapping.
This will serve for light-clients to establish a connection to a node without having to know the discovery.

16907,IssuesEvent,2017-03-23 13:02:04+00:00,50646767,raiden-network/raiden,3705643,konradkonrad,19666847,raiden-network,opened,Diagnostics for failure for RPC-API consumer,[],[],0,"## Problem Definition

For consumer of the RPC-API it is not easily possible to introspect the internal state of Raiden through python in case of an error. We need to ensure that it remains possible to find the cause of a failure. Errors under consideration here are:
- failing transfers: what was the state transition that failed
- crashes mid transfer: raiden dies during a pending transfer leads to an unknown result of the transfer
- failing transport/discovery: problems to contact a peer (i.e. wrong discovery information, transport timeout
For [failing transfers](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/440): HTTP status code signals failure. Reasons could be:
- [ ] Lock Expiry/User Cancel Transfer: ends up in the WAL and should be part of the transfer result object.
- [ ] Insufficient Balance: must be part of the transfer result object.
- [ ] an initiator does know about the identifier of the transfer; we could add a ""query transfer by identifier"" endpoint.
- [ ] for mediators and targets: we could add a ""get pending transfers"" endpoint. But it is possible to trace the state from the write ahead event log.
We don't expose these internals through RPC, instead we must ensure that:
- [ ] discovery lookup operations end up in the (DEBUG-) application log with sufficient information (lookup address, resolved endpoint).
- [ ] other socket related errors should be encoded into the application log.",{},{}

17625,IssuesEvent,2015-07-30 22:00:47+00:00,14284859,marshmallow-code/marshmallow,1790313,bearz,10334301,marshmallow-code,opened,Double quote symbol leads to escaping when serializing errors to json,[],[],0,"Hi there!

In [1]: from marshmallow import fields, Schema

17633,IssuesEvent,2015-09-11 02:41:12+00:00,14284859,marshmallow-code/marshmallow,2379650,sloria,10334301,marshmallow-code,closed,QuerySelect fields encourage slow code,[],[],15,"The `_serialize` and `_deserialize` methods of the `QuerySelect` and `QuerySelectList` fields are slow by design and should only be used for small cursors, if at all. Both methods load data (presumably from an ORM) row by row into Python, compare some key of that row against `value`, and continue if the keys are different. This type of work is almost always orders of magnitude faster when pushed to the underlying database: compare iterating over a large cursor in Python versus a `WHERE` query on an indexed column in SQLAlchemy / SQL.

Users may not realize that the implementation of these fields can easily cause poor performance. For example, use of these fields in marshmallow-sqlalchemy is currently causing relationship fields load the entire foreign table on every call to `_serialize` or `_deserialize`. I propose that these fields either add a warning in the documentation explaining that they are unsuitable for large result sets and should be used with care (and a query profiler!), or be removed from core altogether. Reading over the discussion around the original PR, I think this kind of field makes much more sense when written on a per-ORM basis and maintained in a separate library--like marshmallow-sqlalchemy or smore.",{},{}

17886,IssuesEvent,2017-10-24 13:01:50+00:00,32737992,napalm-automation/napalm,16694679,mirceaulinic,16415577,napalm-automation,opened,get_lldp_neighbors_detail() to include remote_port_type',"[{'id': 729393327, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/napalm-automation/napalm/labels/api%20change', 'name': 'api change', 'color': 'fbca04', 'default': False}, {'id': 729310725, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/napalm-automation/napalm/labels/feature', 'name': 'feature', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 190946936, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/napalm-automation/napalm/labels/question', 'name': 'question', 'color': 'cc317c', 'default': True}]",[],0,"### Description of Issue/Question
Not a bug but a proposal for an additional field in the response from get_lldp_neighbors_detail()

Currently the response data model includes ""remote_port"" which is mapped from the 'port ID' LLDP TLV.  However, the Port ID also has a type, 'Port ID type' which can be a number of different things.
The following are the types specified in IEEE's LLDP MIB, [LldpPortIdSubtype](url)

The following example, taken from the mocked data tests, the  'remote_port"" is of MAC address type.  Depending on the LLDP implementation, they may use different types. Arista switches use type 4 (interfaceName), Linux's lldpd uses type 3 (macAddress) while putting in the interface name in the port description TLV. If I remember correctly, NX-OS uses type 7 (local).
So the conclusion is that it would be quite useful to include the 'port id type' as well in the response to give a hint about what information is provided.

As it is right now, If I want to get the remote interface name, I have to apply some logic to try to guess such as regex matching the port ID against expected interface names and if it doesn't match use the remote port description instead.
In the eAPI result, the dictionary key describing the port ID type is called ""interfaceIdType""

Unfortunately, the cli output in IOS for instance, does not include the remote port ID type at all so it could be a challenge to implement for IOS. As far as I know that information can only be retrieved via SNMP from an IOS device, but hopefully I am wrong in that respect.",{},{}

When rendering a ModelForm with a RadioSelect widget for a model field with `null=True`, the null option is not checked even if the default value is set to `None`.
This comes from the fact that Django normally converts the field value from `None` to `''` in the `render()` method:
This conversion is bypassed by django-crispy-forms. When the field is rendered, the value for the option is `''`, and the value for the field is `'None'`, so `checked=""checked""` is not inserted.
<input type=""radio""{% if choice.0|stringformat:""s"" == field.value|stringformat:""s"" %} checked=""checked""{% endif %} ....
Screenshots and other additional info are not included in this issue as a PR is coming.",{},{}

18401,IssuesEvent,2017-03-23 13:01:57+00:00,60025264,Alignak-monitoring-contrib/alignak-webui,2932687,mohierf,14056682,Alignak-monitoring-contrib,opened,Print date/duration being aware of TZ,[],[],0,"When printing a timestamp as a date or duration, we currently have a problem if the server running the WebUI is not in the same time zone as the server running the Alignak backend.
In the Alignak backend history the date is stored as a GMT timestamp:

If the server running the WebUI is having a time zone that is not GMT, the printed information is not correct: **in 12m 29s**, which is in the future whereas the event happended in the past ! This because of the time zone difference
Currently it is recommended to run the WebUI application on a GMT server",{},{}
